text: This approach is effective in 60-70% of cases, requires lengthy fluoroscopic exposure times, and carries a 5-6% complication rate.

The aim of the study was to access the technical feasibility of a subxiphoid approach for pulmonary vein dissection in an acute animal model so as to avoid the complications of endovascular and open surgical approaches.
The procedure started by making a small subxiphoid incision.
Using blunt dissection, the metastatum is accessed through this incision.
A double channel scope is introduced where the external surface of the pericardium, pleura, and lungs are identified.
A small incision is made on the surface of the pericardium using an endoscopic scissor.
This hole is then enlarged with the IT knife, avoiding the beating heart.
This hole is enlarged, allowing the endoscope to pass through.
Upon entering the pericardium, the heart, greater vessels, inferior vena cava are identified.
A hood is placed on the tip of the scope to improve the visualization and identification of the pericardial structures.
Dissection of the pulmonary vein starts by identifying the inferior vena cava.
The inferior vena cava is separated from inferior branches of the pulmonary vein using a flexible Maryland dissector and IT knife.
Once the inferior branch is isolated, the dissection continues through its lateral margin, identifying and dissecting the superior branch of the left pulmonary vein.
Then the right pulmonary vein is exposed, opening the recess of the pericardium lateral to the vena cava from distal to proximal.
Once this recess is opened, the right pulmonary vein is dissected as well as its superior and inferior branch.
After the procedure is completed, the animal is sacrificed for immediate necropsy.
Animals are examined to confirm the anatomic findings.
Here we can see the right pulmonary vein.
This is the inferior branch of the left pulmonary vein and its superior branch.
